title: Natural dishwasher powder
subtitle: A washing soda and citric acid blend for daily machine dishwashing
excerpt: A dry dishwasher powder made from washing soda, citric acid, and bicarbonate of soda. Washing soda cuts through grease; citric acid tackles mineral deposits and acts as a natural rinse agent; bicarbonate deodorises. Use 1-2 tablespoons per cycle in the detergent compartment.
sourceNotes: Soda crystals dishwasher guidance, Dri-Pak Ltd (dri-pak.co.uk, May 2026). Citric acid as a descaler and rinse-aid agent, UK Water Treatment Association notes.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale freely; a 900 g batch fills a 1 L jar and gives 40-60 cycles.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready immediately. Shelf life 2 years in a dry sealed container.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years in a sealed dry container. Keep tightly sealed to prevent moisture absorption and clumping. Any clumping does not affect cleaning performance; break apart before use. Do not store in a damp location.

content.text: A dry dishwasher powder. Three ingredients handle three different cleaning challenges. Washing soda (sodium carbonate) is the primary degreaser and water softener: it raises the pH of the wash water to cut through grease and prevents calcium ions from forming limescale on crockery and inside the machine. Citric acid works in the opposite way; as the water temperature rises and the pH swings, the citric acid dissolves any mineral deposits already present and acts as a natural rinse aid, helping water sheet off dishes cleanly. Bicarbonate of soda deodorises the wash and moderates the final pH.

Mixing a strong acid (citric acid) and a strong base (washing soda) in a dry powder is safe because no reaction occurs without water. Once they dissolve in the hot dishwasher water the reaction is what cleans the machine. Store the powder dry, and keep the container sealed to prevent atmospheric moisture triggering the reaction in the jar.

items.prepNote: washing soda crystals (sodium carbonate / soda crystals). 44% of the powder. The primary degreaser and mineral-deposit preventer.
fine citric acid powder (food-grade). 33% of the powder. Dissolves existing mineral deposits and acts as a rinse agent.
fine bicarbonate of soda. 22% of the powder. Deodorises and balances the final wash pH.

content.text: Weigh all three powders into a large bowl and stir thoroughly with the silicone spatula. The blend will not react in the dry state. Transfer to a sealed glass jar or airtight tin. Label with the contents and batch date.

Add 1-2 tablespoons (15-30 g) to the detergent compartment of the dishwasher and close the latch. Run on any standard cycle. For hard water areas (above 200 ppm), use the higher amount. You can also half-fill the rinse-aid compartment with white vinegar to supplement the citric acid's rinse action; check the dishwasher manual to confirm rinse-aid compartment compatibility.

This powder is suitable for: glazed ceramics, stainless steel cutlery, glass, and most everyday crockery. Not suitable for: unglazed terracotta, antique or hand-painted ceramics (the alkalinity can dull gilding and enamel), cast iron (rust accelerator), and items labelled hand-wash only. Still fill the machine's dedicated salt compartment with dishwasher salt as normal; this powder does not replace the ion-exchange salt function.

2 years in a sealed, dry container away from steam. The key enemy is moisture; even ambient kitchen steam will cause the citric acid and washing soda to react slowly and clump. Keep the lid on tightly. A silica gel packet in the jar helps in a steamy kitchen environment.
